seo.title: Homeowner Resources | BuiltWell CT
seo.description: Guides, checklists, and articles to help Connecticut homeowners plan, budget, and manage remodeling projects. From BuiltWell CT.

data.content: This page is here for homeowners who want to understand what goes into a remodeling project before picking up the phone. We've put together practical information on planning, costs, permits, and how to evaluate a contractor. None of it is a sales pitch. If you read through this and decide another contractor is a better fit, that's fine. What matters is that you go into a remodel informed.

data.title: Plan Your Project
data.content:
Before starting a remodel, Connecticut homeowners need to think through four things: what the project actually includes, what a realistic budget looks like, when the work can happen, and whether permits are required for the scope they have in mind.

That list sounds simple, but each item has more to it than most people expect when they sit down to plan a project for the first time.

Scoping a project well starts with separating what you want from what the space actually needs. A kitchen that looks dated may only need new countertops, paint, and hardware, a cosmetic refresh. Or the layout may not work for how you use it, the electrical panel may be undersized for modern appliances, and the subfloor may need attention before any finish materials go in. Those are two very different projects with very different costs. A contractor who looks at your space carefully before writing a proposal can tell you which situation you're in. A contractor who quotes from a photo cannot.

Cosmetic work such as painting, hardware, light fixtures, and some flooring can often be planned and started quickly. Structural work such as moving walls, adding or relocating plumbing, upgrading electrical, or adding square footage involves permits, licensed trade subcontractors, and longer timelines. It's worth knowing early which category your project falls into, because it affects your budget, your timeline, and your planning.

Timing is a real factor in Connecticut. Custom cabinet lead times run 8 to 12 weeks from order to delivery. If your project needs custom cabinets, that lead time has to be built into the schedule before construction begins. Projects planned in the fall for spring starts need to account for that window. See our process page for how we handle planning and lead times: /process/

Budget ranges vary significantly by service type and project scope. For detailed cost information, see:
- Kitchen remodeling: /kitchen-remodeling/
- Bathroom remodeling: /bathroom-remodeling/
- Basement finishing: /basement-finishing/
- Flooring: /flooring/

Have questions before you're ready to schedule? Our FAQ covers the most common planning questions: /faq/

data.title: Understanding Costs and Timelines
data.content:
Connecticut homeowners should expect to pay anywhere from $15,000 for a basic bathroom update to $150,000 or more for a high-end kitchen remodel, depending on the service, scope, materials, and structural conditions in the home.

The numbers below are general ranges drawn from projects in Fairfield and New Haven County. They're starting points for thinking about budget, not project quotes. Actual costs depend on the specific conditions in your home, your material selections, and the full scope of work. Each service page linked below includes a detailed cost table with scope descriptions.

Kitchen Remodeling: Minor refreshes start around $25,000 to $50,000. Full mid-range remodels with new cabinets, countertops, and appliances run $50,000 to $90,000. High-end projects with custom cabinets, layout changes, or premium appliances range from $90,000 to $150,000 and above. In parts of Fairfield County, full custom projects with imported materials often exceed $150,000. Timelines run 6 to 12 weeks, plus cabinet lead time if custom cabinetry is ordered. Full details: /kitchen-remodeling/

Bathroom Remodeling: Basic updates to fixtures, vanity, and flooring start at $15,000 to $25,000. A full gut-and-rebuild of a standard bathroom runs $25,000 to $55,000. Primary bathrooms with custom tile, walk-in showers, or premium fixtures range from $55,000 to $80,000. Layout changes and expansions push costs to $80,000 to $120,000 and above. Timelines run 3 to 6 weeks. Full details: /bathroom-remodeling/

Basement Finishing: Basic finishing with framing, drywall, standard flooring, and lighting starts at $25,000 to $45,000. Projects with multiple rooms, upgraded flooring, and bathroom rough-in run $45,000 to $70,000. Adding a full bathroom, wet bar, or custom built-ins pushes projects to $70,000 to $100,000 and above. Per-square-foot costs run $30 to $75 depending on scope. Timelines run 4 to 8 weeks. Full details: /basement-finishing/

Flooring: Installed costs range from $4 to $25 per square foot depending on material. LVP runs $6 to $14 installed, engineered hardwood $8 to $18, solid hardwood $12 to $25, tile $12 to $25, and carpet $4 to $12. Most flooring projects complete in 2 to 5 days. Full details: /flooring/

One cost factor that surprises many Connecticut homeowners: older homes frequently have conditions inside walls and floors that aren't visible until construction begins. Pre-1980 homes may have undersized electrical panels, galvanized plumbing, asbestos-containing floor tile, or subfloor damage from old water intrusion. A thorough pre-construction assessment reduces surprises, but any experienced contractor will tell you that some conditions only become visible once work starts. Written change orders before any additional work proceeds are the standard practice for managing this honestly.

data.title: Permits and Building Codes in Connecticut
data.content:
Connecticut requires permits for most structural and mechanical remodeling work, including framing changes, electrical upgrades, plumbing modifications, egress window installations, and basement finishing projects that create habitable space.

Here is what you need to know as a homeowner.

What typically requires a permit in Connecticut: Any work that changes the structure of a home, including removing or adding walls. Electrical work beyond simple fixture swaps, including new circuits, panel upgrades, and subpanel installation. Plumbing work beyond fixture replacements, including new drain lines, supply line relocations, and rough-in for new bathrooms. HVAC additions or modifications. Egress window installations (required in basement bedrooms by building code). Basement finishing projects that create habitable rooms.

What typically does not require a permit: Cosmetic work such as painting, flooring over an existing subfloor, cabinet replacement without plumbing or electrical changes, and countertop replacement.

Who handles permits on a BuiltWell project: We prepare and submit all permit applications for every project we manage. We account for the permit review timeline in the construction schedule before giving you a start date. We manage all required inspections through the life of the project and coordinate with the building department directly. You do not need to interact with the building department unless you choose to.

Why unpermitted work matters at resale: Unpermitted work is a disclosure issue in Connecticut real estate transactions. If a previous owner or contractor completed work without a permit, it may need to be opened, inspected, and approved before a sale can close, at the current owner's expense. Unpermitted electrical or structural work can also affect homeowner's insurance coverage for claims related to that work. The short-term savings from skipping permits create real problems down the line.

Verifying a contractor's license: Any contractor performing home improvement work in Connecticut for compensation over $200 is required by state law to hold a Home Improvement Contractor (HIC) license issued by the Connecticut Department of Consumer Protection. You can verify any contractor's license at the CT DCP online license lookup. Our license number is CT HIC #0668405; look it up and confirm it's current before we talk.

data.title: How to Choose a Remodeling Contractor
data.content:
The most important questions to ask before hiring a Connecticut contractor involve verifying credentials, understanding communication practices, and reading the written proposal carefully before signing anything.

We've been in this industry long enough to hear what goes wrong. The problems homeowners describe most often are not about the physical work; they're about contractors who stopped communicating, missed the schedule without explanation, delivered something different from what was discussed, or disappeared when issues needed to be resolved. Here is a practical checklist for evaluating any contractor, including us.

Verify the CT HIC license. Ask for the license number and look it up at the Connecticut Department of Consumer Protection. A current, valid license means the contractor has met the state's requirements and has a traceable legal identity. An unlicensed contractor leaves you with fewer legal protections if something goes wrong.

Confirm insurance coverage. Ask for a certificate of general liability insurance and a workers' compensation certificate. Both should be current. If a worker is injured on your property and the contractor doesn't carry workers' compensation, you can be exposed to a liability claim. This is not a theoretical concern; it happens.

Ask for a written, line-item proposal. A single number on a piece of paper is not a proposal. A legitimate proposal describes the full scope of work: what's included and what's not, how long the project will take, what permits are required, and what the payment schedule looks like. Vague language in a proposal becomes a source of disagreement later. If a contractor won't provide a line-item scope document, that tells you something.

Check reviews on Google and other platforms. Look at the volume and recency of reviews, not just the star rating. Read the one- and two-star reviews carefully. Pay attention to how the contractor responds to negative reviews; that response often tells you more about how they operate than the positive reviews do.

Ask about daily communication. Construction projects move fast, and conditions inside walls and floors can change scope quickly. Ask how the contractor communicates during construction: who is your point of contact, how often will you hear from them, and how are change orders handled. Daily updates are the standard we hold ourselves to. Not everyone does.

Understand payment terms before signing. Most Connecticut remodeling contracts involve a deposit, one or more progress payments, and a final payment on completion. Be cautious of any contractor who asks for more than 30 to 40 percent upfront, or who asks for final payment before the walkthrough is complete.

Ask who actually does the work. Some contracting companies sell the job and subcontract everything to whoever is available. Others maintain consistent crews. Consistent crews tend to produce more consistent work and are more invested in the schedule. Ask directly.

data.title: Our Service Areas
data.content:
We serve homeowners throughout Fairfield County and New Haven County, Connecticut. Our crews work across both counties, from communities along the coast to inland towns.

Fairfield County service area, covering Greenwich, Westport, Darien, New Canaan, Stamford, Norwalk, Fairfield, Ridgefield, and surrounding communities: /fairfield-county/

New Haven County service area, covering Orange, New Haven, Hamden, Milford, Branford, Guilford, Madison, Woodbridge, and surrounding communities: /new-haven-county/

Full list of areas we serve: /areas-we-serve/

If you're not sure whether we cover your area, contact us directly. We'll tell you straightforwardly.
